questions.question: What does it mean that relational algebra operators satisfy the 'closure property'?
options: Every operator can only be applied once in a query
Every operator takes one or more relations as input and produces a relation as output
Every operator closes over the schema and prevents changes to it
Every operator closes the relation to further modifications
questions.explanation: Closure means every RA operator (σ, π, ⋈, etc.) takes relation(s) and returns a relation — so you can compose them arbitrarily into trees. This is what makes query optimization (tree rewriting) possible.

questions.question: In the relational model, what is the fundamental difference between a relation and a SQL table?
options: Relations have rows and columns; tables do not
Relations are sets of tuples (no duplicates); SQL tables are bags (multisets) by default
Relations can only have one column; tables can have many
There is no difference — the terms are interchangeable
questions.explanation: The relational model defines relations as sets of tuples — duplicates are impossible. SQL tables default to bag semantics, allowing duplicates unless DISTINCT is specified. This gap causes many subtle bugs.

questions.question: Which relational algebra operator(s) correspond to a SQL query like: SELECT DISTINCT product FROM orders WHERE amount > 100;
options: σ only
π only
δ(π(σ(...))) — duplicate elimination, project, then select
π(σ(...)) — project after select, where π already eliminates duplicates
questions.explanation: SQL's DISTINCT makes this set semantics (δ in RA), but π in the relational model already eliminates duplicates. So π(σ(Orders)) is correct in pure RA. In bag SQL, SELECT product (without DISTINCT) is a bag projection that preserves duplicates, so δ or DISTINCT is needed to get set semantics.

questions.question: What does Codd's Rule 9 (Logical Data Independence) require of a relational DBMS?
options: The database must work the same whether deployed on one server or many
Applications must be unaffected by changes to the logical structure as long as the information is preserved
All data must be represented as values in table cells
NULLs must be handled as missing information, not as actual values
questions.explanation: Rule 9 says you can restructure tables (e.g., decompose a table via normalization) and existing applications should still work, because views or queries can reconstruct the original structure. Rule 8 covers physical independence (storage changes); Rule 11 covers distribution.

questions.question: A query plan shows: HashAggregate on GROUP BY dept_id, then Seq Scan on employees. Which RA operator does HashAggregate implement?
options: σ (select)
π (project)
γ (aggregation)
τ (sort)
questions.explanation: HashAggregate is PostgreSQL's implementation of γ (aggregation) — it groups tuples by dept_id and computes aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, etc.) per group. σ would appear as a Filter node; π appears in the target list; τ appears as Sort.

questions.question: Which RA operator would you use to find all students who have taken EVERY course offered (i.e., the set of students where there is no course they haven't taken)?
options: θ-join (⋈ₚ)
Aggregation with COUNT (γ)
Division (÷) — typically expressed as π_student(Student ⋈ Enrollment) ÷ π_course(Courses)
Natural join (⋈)
questions.explanation: Division (÷) finds tuples in R that match every tuple in S over common attributes. It's the RA operation behind the 'all'/'every' quantifier. SQL implements it via double NOT EXISTS or a COUNT/HAVING pattern: π_student(Enrollment) ÷ π_course(Courses).
